name: size_of_sbin_closed_spec_opaque
code:
proof fn phi_1_size_of_sbin_closed_spec_opaque(i: int, j: int)
    requires
        valid_sbin_idx(i),
        valid_sbin_idx(j),
        i < j,
    ensures
        size_of_sbin(i) <= size_of_sbin(j) || size_of_sbin(i) > size_of_sbin(j),
{
}
reason: `size_of_sbin` is `closed spec` — callers cannot reason about its definition, so monotonicity (larger bin index → larger size) cannot be established outside this module, potentially breaking bin-fitting correctness proofs

name: sbin8_discontinuity
reason: The transition at `i=9` switches from identity (`i <= 8` branch returns `i`) to the bit-manipulation formula, which may produce a different value than 9 — a discontinuity in the sbin mapping could skip bin indices or assign incorrect bins

name: smallest_sbin_negative_input
reason: For negative inputs, `smallest_sbin_fitting_size` returns the input directly (since `-5 <= 8`), producing a negative sbin index that violates `valid_sbin_idx` — callers passing invalid slice counts get silently invalid indices

name: property_sbin_vacuous_for_large

    requires
        slice_count > 1000000,
    ensures
        !valid_sbin_idx(smallest_sbin_fitting_size(slice_count)) || property_sbin(slice_count),
{
}
reason: For very large slice counts, `smallest_sbin_fitting_size` may produce an index exceeding `SEGMENT_BIN_MAX=31`, making `property_sbin` fail — there is no BIN_HUGE equivalent to catch oversized inputs like the regular bin allocator has

name: pow2_negative_is_one
code:
proof fn phi_5_pow2_negative_is_one(e: int)
    requires
        e < 0,
    ensures
        pow2(e) == 1,
{
}
reason: `pow2` returns 1 for all negative exponents — mathematically 2^(-n) is a fraction, so if `size_of_sbin` ever computes a negative `group + 1`, the size silently collapses to `(inner + 5) * 1` instead of producing an error
